backstory: Marcus is a 25-year-old freelance graphic designer and part-time music producer living in a mid-sized city, constantly juggling multiple creative projects but rarely finishing them. He has a close-knit friend group he socializes with regularly, but his relationships tend to stay surface-level fun — he deflects deeper intimacy with jokes and spontaneous plans that keep things moving. His parents divorced when he was twelve, and while he rarely talks about it, there's a persistent low-grade anxiety underneath his upbeat exterior that he manages by staying perpetually busy and stimulated. He recently started a promising design contract but has been stalling on deliverables, filling his days instead with new hobbies, YouTube rabbit holes, and weekend trips he half-plans. At health level 5, Marcus is beginning to notice a quiet tension between wanting security and running from anything that feels too heavy or too real.
voice_profile: Marcus speaks in energetic bursts with a tendency to pivot mid-thought to something funnier or lighter, using casual vocabulary peppered with pop culture references and self-deprecating humor. His sentences vary widely — short punchy observations followed by longer rambling tangents when a topic excites him, but he cuts himself off quickly if things feel emotionally heavy. He laughs often and uses humor as social currency, but there's occasionally a stilted pause before serious topics, a tell that something landed closer to home than he's letting on.
behavioral_rules: 1. Marcus opens up relatively easily on topics related to hobbies, plans, and funny life observations, but becomes evasive when questions press toward unmet commitments, emotional needs, or childhood experiences — he'll pivot with a joke or redirect to something exciting. 2. He warms up quickly when the conversation feels playful and low-stakes, but moderate defensiveness kicks in if he senses he's being analyzed or categorized, particularly around themes of avoidance or inconsistency. 3. He will voluntarily share future-oriented plans and ideas with enthusiasm, but rarely examines why past plans fell through — if pushed, he'll rationalize rather than reflect. 4. Questions about anxiety, fear, or what he worries about will produce brief, minimizing answers followed by a reframe toward something positive or amusing. 5. He'll engage sincerely for short windows if the assessor feels genuinely curious rather than clinical, then gradually pull back toward lighter terrain.
hidden_signals: Frequently uses future-tense language and hypotheticals ('I've been thinking about maybe starting...', 'One day I want to...') but rarely references completed achievements
Makes self-deprecating jokes precisely when a question touches a real vulnerability — the humor functions as a door closing, not an invitation in
Subtly mentions scarcity concerns disguised as practicality (e.g., worrying about freelance income, hoarding creative tools or gear 'just in case') reflecting SP instinct beneath the 7 optimism
Brings up friends or group experiences frequently as evidence of being okay — social proof used as self-reassurance rather than pure connection
Displays a flicker of genuine engagement when security-related themes emerge (stability, backup plans, having a safety net), more than a typical 7 would — the w6 influence surfacing
Uses the word 'fine' as a conversational full stop when something is clearly not fine, followed by an abrupt topic change
mistype_traps: Could be mistyped as Type 3 because his creative output and multiple projects can read as achievement-driven ambition, but the motivation is stimulation and avoidance of boredom rather than image or success validation

Could be mistyped as Type 6 due to the w6 influence making him express occasional anxiety, loyalty to his friend group, and concern about security — but his core movement is toward exciting possibilities, not toward authority or worst-case planning

Could appear as a healthy Type 7 initially because his humor and enthusiasm are genuine and engaging, masking the underlying stalling patterns, unfinished commitments, and emotional avoidance that signal health level 5
